mappings.value: One equals K times the integral from minus pi over 2 to pi over 2 of cosine squared theta times g of K r sine theta d theta, on the nonzero branch r greater than zero. Here K is the coupling strength, r is the order parameter, and g is the natural-frequency distribution evaluated at the self-consistent argument K r sine theta.

Equation 12 comes from dividing Eq. 11 by r, so it describes only the synchronized branch with r greater than zero. Its job is to locate onset by asking when that nonzero branch first appears out of the incoherent state.

Taking the limit r goes to zero from the positive side turns Eq. 12 into the threshold formula K sub c equals two over pi g of zero. That is why Eq. 12 is the general critical-coupling equation before any special choice of g.
Use Eq. 12 when you want the onset condition for a general smooth even frequency distribution. The detailed walkthrough to K sub c lives in the Eq. 12 to Eq. 13 derivation.
